name: Hiranyakashipu
description: Hiranyakashipu is a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, renowned as a powerful Daitya king who sought immortality and dominion over the universe. Born to Diti and Kashyapa, his intense devotion and rigorous asceticism granted him a boon from Brahma, bestowing near-invulnerability. This boon stipulated that he could not be killed by human or animal, inside or outside, during the day or night, on earth or in the sky, or by any weapon. Arrogant and tyrannical, Hiranyakashipu forbade the worship of Vishnu, whom he considered his mortal enemy after Vishnu slew his brother Hiranyaksha. He demanded that all worship him as the supreme god. His reign was marked by oppression and a relentless pursuit of power, challenging the cosmic order and provoking divine intervention. Ultimately, his hubris led to his demise at the hands of Vishnu's Narasimha avatar, a being neither man nor beast, who circumvented the conditions of Brahma's boon.
subtitle: The Golden-Clad Demon King

keyMyths.title: Hiranyakashipu's Boon
keyMyths.description: Driven by a desire for immortality and revenge against Vishnu, Hiranyakashipu performed intense austerities for thousands of years. His penance was so powerful that it threatened the balance of the universe, compelling Brahma to grant him a boon. Hiranyakashipu cleverly requested a boon that made him nearly invulnerable, specifying conditions that he believed would make him immortal, thus securing his reign over the cosmos and enabling his tyrannical rule without fear of death.

keyMyths.title: The Persecution of Prahlada
keyMyths.description: Hiranyakashipu's son, Prahlada, was a staunch devotee of Vishnu, which enraged his father. Despite repeated attempts to dissuade him and numerous cruel punishments, Prahlada's unwavering devotion remained steadfast. Hiranyakashipu's frustration grew with each failed attempt to kill Prahlada, highlighting the power of devotion and the limitations of even the most potent boons and tyrannical power. This conflict became a central theme in the narrative.

keyMyths.title: The Appearance of Narasimha
keyMyths.description: When Hiranyakashipu challenged Prahlada to prove Vishnu's omnipresence by showing him in a pillar, Vishnu manifested as Narasimha, an avatar that was neither man nor beast. Narasimha emerged from the pillar at twilight (neither day nor night), placed Hiranyakashipu on his lap (neither earth nor sky), and disemboweled him with his claws (neither weapon nor hand). This circumvented the conditions of Brahma's boon, fulfilling Vishnu's purpose to restore dharma and protect his devotee.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: Provides detailed accounts and interpretations of Hindu myths, including the story of Hiranyakashipu and Narasimha.

sources.author: F. Max Müller
sources.description: Offers insights into the philosophical and religious context of the stories, shedding light on the concepts of dharma, karma, and devotion.

sources.author: Devdutt Pattanaik
sources.work: Myth=Mithya: A Handbook of Hindu Mythology
sources.description: Explores the symbolic meanings and cultural significance of Hindu myths, including the narrative of Hiranyakashipu's downfall.
